coredata.dc:title: The fabrication of fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection and configurable logic gate operation based on N-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanan
Ji, Xinxin
Liang, Jiamin
Gao, Yue
Xing, Huan
Song, Yuxin
Hou, Juan
Yang, Guang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained using hydrothermal treatment of polyethyleneimine and melamine. The N-CDs exhibit nanoscale structure, good water solubility, photostability and excellent fluorescence property with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 26 %. The as-prepared N-CDs were selected for the detection of Fe3+ with a fluorescence “OFF-ON” strategy by the mechanism of internal filtration effect (IFE). A good linear relationship can be observed for the concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 500 μM, and the calculated detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ was 0.88 μM (3σ/slope). Meanwhile, the N-CDs based sensor can be utilized for tracing Fe3+ in real water samples. In addition, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered with the treatment of l-cysteine, and thus can be designed for quantitative determination of l-cysteine with a fluorescent “ON-OFF-ON” switch. Moreover, a set of resettable, multireadout logic gate operations including OR and AND have been realized for the N-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Fe3+ detection
Internal filtration effect
Logic gate operation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image and particle size distribution of N-CDs. intensity ratio of ID/IG, which was calculated to be 0.94.
(a) Entire XPS spectrum, and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of the N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (inset photos of the N-CDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm light irradiation (right), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths.
(a) The fluorescence response of the N-CDs with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 ∼ 500 μM, (b) linear relationship between the lg(F0/F) and the Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 ∼ 500 μM, λexi: 340 nm.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity after adding different metal ions of as-synthesized N-CDs; (b) The effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the presence of other metal ions (500 μM).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of N-CDs; (b) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs solution before and after adding Fe3+.
Truth table for the (a) OR gate and (b) AND gate operation for the N-CDs.
Schematic showing the synthetic route for N-CDs.

head.ce:title: The fabrication of fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection and configurable logic gate operation based on N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained using hydrothermal treatment of polyethyleneimine and melamine. The N-CDs exhibit nanoscale structure, good water solubility, photostability and excellent fluorescence property with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 26 %. The as-prepared N-CDs were selected for the detection of Fe3+ with a fluorescence “OFF-ON” strategy by the mechanism of internal filtration effect (IFE). A good linear relationship can be observed for the concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 500 μM, and the calculated detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ was 0.88 μM (3σ/slope). Meanwhile, the N-CDs based sensor can be utilized for tracing Fe3+ in real water samples. In addition, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered with the treatment of l-cysteine, and thus can be designed for quantitative determination of l-cysteine with a fluorescent “ON-OFF-ON” switch. Moreover, a set of resettable, multireadout logic gate operations including OR and AND have been realized for the N-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, owing to the technological and economic increase greatly, water pollution is one of the main environmental issues, and it can bring the discharge of wastewater that contains several kinds of heavy metal ions to the environment. It can accumulate in human beings through inhalation from food or water, the contact by skin, or the gastrointestinal tract, thus resulting in a grave threat to human health [1,2]. Among them, ferric ions (Fe3+) play a significant part in maintaining the regular physiological processes of human beings, including the production of antibodies, the binding and transport of oxygen, the arrangement and interchange of respiratory processes and the regulation of genes [3–5]. However, insufficient or excessive Fe3+ can lead to several illnesses, such as liver, lung or heart diseases, anemia, decreased immunity or malignant tumors [6–10]. In addition, the maximum permitted concentration of Fe3+ in water was 0.3 mg/mL, which was promulgated by our country in 2007. Therefore, the design of a facile, sensitive and rapid method for detecting Fe3+ ions is highly demanded.

Although several traditional approaches for detecting Fe3+ ion including atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry, colorimetry and electrochemical methods [11–15], have been developed with high sensitivity and accuracy, these methods require expensive instruments, professional analysts, cumbersome sample pre-treatment and demanding detection conditions, which are not convenient enough to be universally applicable to field detection. Compared with traditional analytical methods, the rapid development of fluorescent detection methods has gained tremendous attention owing to their unique optical properties, facile operation, fast response speed, convenience and good reproducibility [16–20].

Recently, increasing research interest focused on quasizero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) has surged exponentially with time, because of their diverse advantages such as simple preparation, high aqueous-phase solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and outstanding photoluminescence [21–25]. Since their first discovery in 2004, abundant CD-based sensors have been developed for the detection of Fe3+ ions [26–35]. For example, Liu et al. described a novel method for the preparation of CDs from rose-heart radish with a fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 13.6 %. The Fe3+ ion can quench its fluorescence emission and the detection limit (LOD) was 0.13 μM [26]. Atchudan et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs with a QY of 14 %, and the fluorescence emission of N-CDs was inhibited by adding Fe3+ ions with a LOD of 0.9 μM [27]. Sahu et al. synthesized dual-emissive CDs with fluorescence emission at 490 nm and 570 nm. Interestingly, in the presence of Fe3+, the emission intensity at 490 nm reduced and the emission intensity at 570 nm could be enhanced, thus, an obvious fluorescence color transition from light green to bright yellow can be observed with a with a LOD of 31.4 nM for Fe3+ ion detection [28]. Diethylenetriamine-β-cyclodextrin-functionalized CDs (3 N-CQDs) were synthesized by Yang et al. with a higher relative QY of 67.2 %. The as-prepared fluorescent probe displays a sensitive response for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection with LODs of was 0.25 μM and 0.57 μM, respectively [29]. Chen et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CDs by using cysteine and ethylenediamine as precursors, and the as-designed fluorescent sensor displayed sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ with fluorescence quenched via the internal filtration effect [30]. Zhao and colleagues synthesized N, S co-doped CDs by using malic acid and allyl thiourea as precursors with a QY of 19.8 %. The as-prepared NS-CDs can be chosen for fluorescent detection of Ce4+ and Fe3+ with LODs of 0.75 μM and 0.67 μM, respectively [31]. However, above works are based on fluorescence “ON-OFF” strategy, and how to recover the fluorescence of quenched system was not studied. Liu and colleagues synthesized N and S co-doped CDs through one-pot hydrothermal process, and the above N/S-CDs showed a high QY of 26 %. Fluorescence quenching can be realized with the addition of Fe3+ ions, and the LOD of Fe3+ ions can reach at 0.19 μM. Interestingly, the fluorescence can be rapidly recovered after the treatment with [HPO4]2− [32]. Liu et al. designed similar N and S co-doped CDs by a microwave-assisted method, and fluorescence “ON-OFF” can be realized by adding Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ can be recovered by thiol, thus, a fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” can be fabricated for tracing thiol and Fe3+ [33]. Guan et al. synthesized carbon quantum dots (MP-CQDs) by using Mopan persimmons as a carbon source, and the MP-CQDs displayed sensitivity for Fe3+ ion sensing upon the fluorescence quenching process with a LOD of 0.324 μM. Moreover, EDTA shows a higher affinity for Fe3+ ions, thus leading to fluorescence recovery of the fluorescence quenched MP-CQDs system [34]. Despite the above efforts, however, several drawbacks still suffered such as the quenched fluorescence cannot be totally recovered, poor selectivity for fluorescence recovery and lower linear range. Therefore, the strategy for the detection of Fe3+ based on fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” with quantitative analysis of fluorescent recovery substance is still need to be further studied.

Herein, an effective and facile strategy for preparing N-CDs by a hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine and melamine as precursors was proposed. The as-prepared N-CDs display good water solubility, excellent fluorescent properties, and it can be utilized as fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ through the internal filtration effect (IFE) focusing on fluorescence “ON”-“OFF” strategy. More strikingly, the fluorescence of N-CDs/Fe3+ can be totally recovered in the presence of L-cysteine. Based on the above properties, a fluorescence changes that with “ON-OFF-ON” performance was established and several logic gate operations were constructed for N-CDs-based fluorescent platform.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 25000) and melamine were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). Inorganic salts and L-cysteine were obtained from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. in Shanghai. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ) was selected during the experiments.

FT-IR spectra were recorded by using a 400 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was utilized to capture the fluorescence (FL) spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra were captured using a spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU of UV-2550 PC). The fluorescence lifetime was determined using a FLS980 spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments of UK. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using a Netherlands-made FEI TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope. By using a monochromic X-ray source (Al Kaline, 1486.6 eV) and the ESCALAB 250 spectrometer, the XPS spectra were obtained. An XRD-6100 (Shimadzu) was used to capture the X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. To obtain the Raman spectra, a laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (DXR2) was employed.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI) and melamine were chosen as raw materials in the hydrothermal preparation of the N-CDs. Briefly, 2 g of PEI and 0.252 g of melamine were added to a round-bottom flask and dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. After ultrasound treatment for 10 min, the transparent mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL dried Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was purified after being centrifuged for 15 min (10000 rpm). Then, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dialysis for 48 h and dried in a freeze-drying oven for 24 h to obtain yellow N-CDs powders.

Quinine sulfate (QY = 54.6 %) was used as a reference material, and the QY of the N-CDs was calculated as follows:
QY =
Q R
I /
I R
O
D R
/ O D
n 2
/
n R 2

QY refers to the fluorescence quantum yield for the N-CDs, I is the integrated emission intensity of the N-CDs as measured, OD is optical density (typically less than 0.05) detected by UV–vis spectra, and n refers to the refractive index. The standard sample is indicated by subscript R.

N-CD aqueous solutions (pH = 7) were treated with various concentrations of Fe3+ for 60 s, and then record the FL spectra (λexi = 340 nm). The maximum emission intensities at 465 nm were monitor for the quantitative analysis.

Tap water samples were obtained from this library and filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter. Fe3+ were added to the above sample and reacted for 60 s, and the FL spectra were characterized with λexi = 340 nm with a slit width of 5 nm.

The N-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and melamine as precursors and the synthetic pathway for N-CDs is shown in Scheme 1 . The detailed optimization of preparation conditions including reaction time and temperature was provided in Tables S1 and S2, and The QY value was considered to evaluate the N-CDs. The optimal reaction time was 4 h and the optimal reaction temperature was 200 °C. As shown in Fig. 1 , the TEM image of the N-CDs indicates a spherical morphology and the average particle size is 6.5 nm, and there is no evident aggregation. The XRD result in Fig. S1 demonstrates a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 21°, revealing the disordered graphitic structure of the N-CDs [35]. In addition, the graphitization of N-CDs was further studied by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2, two characteristic bands center at 1380 and 1600 cm−1 represent the D and G bands, respectively. The N-CDs' amorphous carbon phase was identified by the FT-IR characterization was chosen to study the typical functional groups of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the FT-IR spectrum shows two characteristic absorption bands centered at 3400 and 3340 cm−1, representing the overlapping of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [34]. The characteristic peaks at 2954 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2- [29]. The absorption bands at 1646, 1541, 1466 and 1127 cm−1 stand for the stretching vibrations of C=O, C=N, C=C and C–O–C, respectively [18,21,29,34].

XPS spectra were used to study the chemical valence state and elemental compositions of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, three elements, C, N, and O, can be observed in the F-PDs, and the characteristic binding energy peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s are centered at 284.6 eV, 398.6 eV, and 531.3 eV, and their percentage compositions are 71.64 %, 12.5 % and 15.98 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s peaks can be fitted into three main types at 284.4, 285.2, and 287.7 eV, representing the C–C/C=C, C–N, and C=N/C=O, respectively (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution of N1s spectrum in Fig. 2c shows two fitted peaks, which stand for the C–N (398.5 eV) and N–H (399.6 eV), respectively. The O1s spectrum contains two peaks, which represent C=O (530.9 eV) and C–O (531.4 eV), respectively (Fig. 2d).
The XPS measurements and FT-IR data are in agreement, indicating that the N element was successfully doped into the CDs.

The UV–vis spectra in Fig. 3 a reveal a typical absorption peak located at 202 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C bond [21]. The colorless N-CDs solution shows fluorescence emission with blue color upon exposure to 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a), and the maximum excitation (λexi) and emission (λem) peaks of the N-CDs are 340 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Using quinine sulfate as the standard, the relative QY of the N-CDs was determined to be 26 %, which is suitable for the design of fluorescent sensors. Meanwhile, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of the N-CDs was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, upon changing the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 370 nm, the emission peak of the N-CDs shows almost the same position without any excitation wavelength dependence. The excitation-independent property indicates that the FL origin of as-prepared N-CDs is the molecular state [36].

Furthermore, the photostability of the N-CDs was evaluated upon measuring the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm with prolonged 365 nm exposureing time. As shown in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm decreased slightly under 365 nm light irradiation, and the intensity value only decreased by approximately 15 % after continuous irradiation for 60 min. The index of salt tolerance is of great significance to evaluate the stability of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4b, the intensity value did not change considerably with increasing the amounts of NaCl, thus endowing them with great potential for fluorescence imaging and sensing. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity shows a negligible change during 30 days of long-term storage at 4 °C, indicating excellent time-stability (Fig. S4c). The fluorescence performance of the N-CDs was tested at different pH conditions. As shown in Fig. S4d, the results show that the N-CDs displays a strong fluorescence emission over a wide range of pH values from 1 to 13, and reach their maximum value at pH = 7 (Fig. S4d). The fluorescence behavior of the N-CDs was tested in different solvents. As shown in Fig. S4e, the solvent display negligible influence on the fluorescence performance of the N-CDs. Moreover, the oxidation resistance ability for N-CDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. S4f, within the concentration of H2O2 increased to 100 mM, the intensity value only decreased by approximately 14 %, and even the amount of increased as high as 325 mM, the FL intensity only decreased 40 %, which indicated satisfactory oxidation resistance ability. All the above findings reveal that the N-CDs had good stability and can be selected in fluorescence analysis and detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection based on the N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-CDs can be utilized as an excellent sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence of N-CDs at 465 nm rapidly diminished and showed a typical concentration-dependent characteristic. When the amount of Fe3+ increased to 500 μM, the quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) was as high as 78 %, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm of N-CDs, and F is the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs after being added with Fe3+. The optimization conditions for Fe3+ detection including pH value, reaction time and temperature was provided in Fig. S5. The optimization pH value was 7, and it shows little effects on Fe3+ detection in the pH value ranges from 3 to 10 (Fig. S5a). As illustrated in Fig. S5b, fluorescence quenching performance could reach equilibrium within 1 min. Meanwhile, the temperature also shows little influence on the quenching efficiency of Fe3+ (Fig. S5c). As shown in Fig. 4b, a good linear relationship can be observed between lg(F0/F) and the concentration of added Fe3+ from 0 to 500 μM, and the linear relationship can be fitted by lg(F0/F) = 0.00133CFe 3+-0.223 (R2 = 0.997). The LOD for Fe3+ detection was calculated as 0.88 μM, which is compared with other published literature in Table S3 and lower than the maximum Fe3+ concentration in drinking water permitted by the “Standards for Drinking Water Quality” [33]. As shown in Fig. S6, the N-CDs display excellent reproducibility for the detection of Fe3+, and the relative standard deviation was 7.3 %, which proved the feasibility and accurate quantification of Fe3+.

The fluorescence quenching effect of other metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Al3+) was also investigated to study the selectivity for Fe3+ detection. As shown in Fig. 5 a, compared with other metal ions, only Fe3+ can lead to a significant fluorescence quenching for N-CDs. We should note here that although Cu2+ can cause a decrease in fluorescence, the influence of Cu2+ can be masked by using EDTA as a chelating agent. In addition, although Fe3+ can also easily form stable complexes with EDTA, larger overlapping region can be observed between the absorbance of Fe3+/EDTA and the excitation spectrum of the N-CDs, which does not influence the quenching process (Fig. S7). Moreover, in the presence of other metal ions coexisting with Fe3+, Fe3+ can still cause fluorescence quenching, and the quenching efficiency cannot be influenced by other metal ions (Fig. 5b). All the above findings indicate that the N-CDs based sensing platform can be designed for detecting Fe3+ with relatively high selectivity. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence can be nearly totally recovered with the addition of l-cysteine, thus, a fluorescent switch with “ON-OFF-ON” can be designed (Fig. S8a). As shown in Fig. S8b, the FL intensity at 465 nm gradually recovered with increasing l-cysteine amount, and the FL intensity value of N-CDs + Fe3+ system reached approximately 95.7 %. The linear regression equation of FL value versus l-cysteine amount is F = 58.5748C + 449.6697 (R2 = 0.994), and the LOD for l-cysteine was calculated to be 465 nM (S/N = 3). Compared with other amino acids, only l-cysteine can realized FL intensity recovery for the N-CDs + Fe3+ system (Fig. S9). All the above results indicate that the N-CDs + Fe3+ system can be utilized for the quantitative determination of l-cysteine.

The mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was studied. As shown in Fig. 6 a, a partial overlap between the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Fe3+ and the excitation spectrum of N-CDs can be observed, thus indicating that the possible mechanism for the fluorescence might be ascribed to the internal filtration effect (IFE) process [18,30]. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. S7, with the treatment of Fe3+, no additional peaks appeared in the absorption in the UV spectrum, which demonstrating the formation of excited state complex [37]. To further research the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime was recorded before and after treatment with Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs was 5.75 ns, however, the fluorescence lifetime displayed a negligible change from 5.75 to 5.45 ns after adding 30 μM Fe3+, thus demonstrating a static quenching process [30]. Furthermore, zeta potential of N-CDs was measured to be −41.5 mV, and the potentials value changed to −5.6 mV after treating with Fe3+, thus indicating that Fe3+ could be adsorbed on the surface of N-CDs upon intermolecular electrostatic interactions (Fig. S10) [29,30]. Overall, focusing above discussions, the actual mechanism responsible for the sensor would be static quenching along with inner filter effect [38,39]. Moreover, with the addition of l-cysteine to the N-CDs + Fe3+ system, the overlap area between the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Fe3++N-CDs + l-cysteine and the excitation spectrum of N-CDs would be decreased (Fig. S11), owing to the stronger binding force between sulfhydryl (-SH) on the surface of l-cysteine and Fe3+, the N-CDs can be released from Fe3+ and lead to the fluorescence of quenched N-CDs system restored, which is similar to the previous reported work [33].

To explore the practicability and reliability of N-CDs for detecting Fe3+ in real water samples, the conventional addition method was selected to analyze the amount of Fe3+ in tap water, snow water and Songhua river water samples. As shown in Tables S4–S6, the RSD values changed from 3.36 % to 4.24 % and the recoveries ranged from 101.72 % to 102.21 % for tap water. The RSD values changed from 3.67 % to 4.57 % and the recoveries ranged from 96.32 % to 102.73 % for snow water. The RSD values changed from 3.83 % to 5.27 % and the recoveries ranged from 97.46 % to 103.70 % for Songhua river sample. Therefore, this method was suitable for tracing Fe3+ in real water samples.

The Boolean logic operation in chemical systems is of great value in the construction of memory devices for the information storage with a write-read-erasable form. Therefore, a set of logic gate operations were realized based on the above results. First, an “OR” logic gate was constructed by taking the N-CDs with bright blue fluorescence as the initial state. Two triggers including Fe3+ and Cu2+ as the two inputs, and the presence or absence of either of them were defined as “1” and “0”. For outputs, the N-CDs with and without fluorescence emissions were defined as “0” and “1”. As shown in Fig. 7 a, without the addition of Fe3+ or Cu2+ (input: 0/0), bright fluorescence emission remained for the N-CDs, thus, the output was “0”. However, after the addition of Fe3+ or Cu2+, regardless of whether one or both of them were added (input: 1/0, 0/1, and 1/1), the N-CDs displayed quenched fluorescence, and the output was “0”. Meanwhile, another “AND” logical operation can be constructed by taking the N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ as the initial state, l-cysteine and EDTA as the two inputs, and the presence or absence of either of them defined as “1” and “0”. For outputs, the N-CDs with fluorescence “ON” was “1”, and fluorescence “OFF” was “0”. As shown in Fig. 7b, owing to the treatment with Fe3+ and Cu2+, the initial state of the N-CDs was fluorescence “OFF”. Therefore, without adding l-cysteine and EDTA (input: 0/0), the output was “0″. However, with the addition of l-cysteine to the initial system (input: 1/0), Fe3+ can be captured by l-cysteine, but the Cu2+ can also lead to fluorescence in the “OFF” state (output: 0). Similarly, with only EDTA treatment (input: 0/1), the influence of Cu2+ can be masked by EDTA, but the Fe3+ can also lead to fluorescence in the “OFF” state (output: 0). Fluorescence with “ON” could be realized (output: 1) only in the presence of both inputs (1/1).

In summary, fluorescent N-CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and melamine as precursors. The N-CDs display an average structure and blue color fluorescence emission with a QY of 26 %. The fluorescence emissions of the N-CDs can be quenched with the treatment of Fe3+ through the IFE mechanism, thus a sensitive fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+ was designed with the advantages of selectivity, high sensitivity, wide detection range and low LOD. The N-CDs based sensor can be used for tracing Fe3+ in real water samples with satisfactory results. In addition, a fluorescent switch with “ON-OFF-ON” can be designed because the fluorescence quenched system can be recovered with the addition of l-cysteine. Moreover, multiple logical operations including OR and AND have been realized successfully, which may pave the way for the rational design of smart materials for information computing techniques.
The datasets used or analyzed in this work are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
Not applicable.
Consent to participate was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Consent for publication was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
The article does not contain any experiments with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

Yanan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Xinxin Ji: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiamin Liang: Data curation, Supervision. Yue Gao: Writing – review & editing. Huan Xing: Writing – review & editing. Yuxin Song: Writing – review & editing. Juan Hou: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Guang Yang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU05) and the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2020E007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115418.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection and removal of heavy metal ions: a review
Environ. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Malik, A. Bashir, A. Qureashi, A.H. Pandith, Detection and removal of heavy metal ions: a review, Environmental Chemistry Letters 17(4) (2019) 1495-1521. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10311-019-00891-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metals and human health: possible exposure pathways and the competition for protein binding sites
Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Witkowska, J. Słowik, K. Chilicka, Heavy Metals and Human Health: Possible Exposure Pathways and the Competition for Protein Binding Sites, Molecules 26(19) (2021) 6060. https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules26196060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-functional fluorescent chemosensor with large stokes shift based on 2D poly(3-amino-carbazole) for efficient p-nitroaniline and Fe3+ detection
Eur. Polym. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.K. Zhang, F. Xu, Q.Y. Lu, R. Zhang, J.B.
